---
title: "Supplementary: COPASI, VCell, and the whole-cell approach — a framework comparison"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    monofont: "Menlo"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    include-in-header: ../../pandoc-header.tex
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    # reference-doc: ../thesis-reference.docx  # optional Word style template
bibliography: ../references.bib
# csl: ../harvard-cite-them-right.csl  # add Harvard CSL (see references.bib)
---

## Purpose and framing

The platelet Ca²⁺ model could have been built with several mature tools, and
meaningful work was achievable with any of them. This note sets out, as
dispassionately as I can, three things: what building this model in **COPASI**
[@hoops2006] or **VCell** [@blinov2008] would have required; what each tool
would have given in return; and why the **whole-cell** approach derived from
*wcEcoli* [@karr2012; @macklin2020] was ultimately chosen. The argument is
about long-term architectural fit, not about any tool being inadequate — at
the current scale, the better-tooled choice for the calcium pathway alone is
arguably COPASI or VCell, not the path I took.

## What the model is, computationally

The calcium model is roughly 50 species distributed across well-mixed
compartments (cytosol, dense tubular system [DTS], mitochondria, plasmalemma,
extracellular space), integrated as ordinary differential equations on a
one-second outer timestep, driven by agonist forcing and validated against
Dolan & Diamond's resting state and IP₃-transient experiment [@dolan2014]. It
is presently **non-spatial** (no Ca²⁺ microdomains). Crucially, the *intended*
end state is not this pathway in isolation but a whole-cell substrate to which
granule release, integrin activation and metabolism attach as further
processes sharing one mass-balanced state. That ambition, more than any
current requirement, drives the comparison below.

## The three approaches at a glance

| Dimension | COPASI | VCell | This work (wcEcoli-derived) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Paradigm | Single kinetic network | Single network in a geometry | Integrated multi-process cell |
| Spatial resolution | Well-mixed compartments | Well-mixed **or** spatial PDE / particle | Well-mixed (by design, for now) |
| Model definition | GUI + SBML | GUI + database; rule-based (BioNetGen) | Plain Python code |
| Copy-number / mass balance | Concentrations, per network | Concentrations / particles, per network | First-class proteome counts across compartments |
| Solvers | Mature deterministic + stochastic | Mature deterministic, stochastic, spatial | `scipy` `solve_ivp` (BDF) |
| Parameter estimation | First-class | Available | Manual / coupled calibration |
| Standards / interop | SBML → BioModels | SBML, SED-ML, central DB | None (no SBML export) |
| Reproducibility | `.cps` / SBML files | Server-stored models | Git, tests, CI (code review) |
| Multi-process integration | — | — | Native (the whole point) |
| Best-fit use case | Well-mixed kinetics + estimation | Spatial / microdomain biology | Extensible whole-cell substrate |

## Building it in COPASI

**What it would take.** Define each compartment with a volume; enter the ~50
species; encode every reaction with a kinetic law (mass action, plus custom
rate laws for the Li–Rinzel IP3R, the SERCA and PMCA enzymatic cycles, and the
STIM1/Orai1 SOCE flux); represent agonist time courses as events or
interpolated assignment rules; calibrate against the Dolan transients using
COPASI's parameter-estimation task; then run time-courses and parameter scans.

**What COPASI would give.** Decades of validated solver engineering —
deterministic (LSODA-class) and exact/approximate stochastic integration in
one package — together with genuinely first-class parameter estimation,
optimisation, sensitivity analysis and metabolic control analysis. SBML
import/export [@keating2020] would make the model depositable to curated
repositories such as BioModels [@maliksheriff2020], and the GUI plus Python
scripting bindings lower the barrier to reproducing and extending it.

**Where it falls short for this project.** COPASI is well-mixed, which is fine
*now* but forecloses microdomain biology later. More fundamentally, it models
**one network**: there is no notion of a proteome-wide copy-number budget or
of several processes sharing and partitioning a common state. Growing toward
granules and integrins would mean an ever-larger monolithic reaction set,
increasingly hard to modularise — precisely the combinatorial-complexity
problem the field has long flagged [@blinov2008].

## Building it in VCell

**What it would take.** Define structures (compartments) and the membranes
between them (plasmalemma, DTS membrane, mitochondrial membranes); place
volume reactions and membrane fluxes (PMCA, Orai1/SOCE, P2X1, MCU) on the
appropriate structures; choose a *compartmental* application for a well-mixed
run, or a *spatial* application with image-derived geometry; calibrate; and
simulate deterministically, stochastically, or — for combinatorial signalling
— via rule-based network generation [@blinov2008].

**What VCell would give.** Everything COPASI offers at the well-mixed level,
plus the capabilities that make it the strongest *single-model* fit for this
biology: native spatial reaction–diffusion with realistic geometry, membrane
electrophysiology, and particle-based stochastic simulation, all behind a
mature GUI with a central model database [cite: VCell — catalogue in Zotero].
This matters here for a concrete reason worth conceding plainly: the SOCE
mechanism the model reproduces is **intrinsically spatial** — STIM1 and Orai1
assemble into puncta at DTS–plasmalemma junctions [@dolan2014]. Should the
platelet model ever need explicit microdomains or puncta geometry, VCell could
represent natively what the current well-mixed formulation only lumps. That is
a real capability the chosen approach gives up.

**Where it falls short for this project.** Despite that power, VCell still
models a **single reaction network within a geometry**. It offers no
whole-cell copy-number accounting and no multi-process scheduling, and models
live in VCell's database and formats rather than as plain code under version
control. The integrative bookkeeping the whole-cell goal requires sits outside
its design centre.

## What the whole-cell approach gives — and costs

The *wcEcoli*-derived framework [@karr2012; @macklin2020] supplies exactly what
the two dedicated tools do not: a multi-process architecture in which
processes declare resource requests, a partitioning step allocates shared
molecules, processes evolve state, and listeners record — with copy-number and
mass balance tracked as first-class quantities across compartments, and many
subsystems able to share one cell state. Because it is plain Python under git,
tests and continuous integration, every parameter and equation is diff-able
and code-reviewable.

The costs are real and should be stated without flinching. The inherited
framework is large and had to be pruned; there is no GUI; the integrator is
general-purpose `scipy` rather than a specialised biochemical solver (adequate
at one-second platelet timescales, but with no built-in parameter-estimation,
metabolic control analysis, or stochastic toolkit); and there is **no SBML
export**, so the model cannot currently be shared through standard repositories
[@keating2020; @maliksheriff2020] without a translation layer. The community
around this exact stack is also small.

## Why this approach

The deciding axis is **scope, not capability at the current scale**. As a
single calibrated calcium pathway, this model would likely have been faster to
build — and better served by parameter estimation and solver maturity — in
COPASI, or in VCell if spatial microdomains were the priority; a prior platelet
model was itself a large single-network ODE system [@purvis2008]. But the
project's aim is a *whole-cell* model: an integrated, mass-balanced,
copy-number-accounted substrate onto which further subsystems attach as
processes sharing one state. That integration is what *wcEcoli* was built for
[@karr2012; @macklin2020] and what neither COPASI nor VCell provides;
reproducing it inside either would mean rebuilding a multi-process whole-cell
scaffold around a tool designed for single networks. Choosing the whole-cell
framework front-loads infrastructure cost to buy architectural fit for the
long-term goal, plus code-level reproducibility — a defensible trade given the
ambition, and an honest one given what is surrendered: mature solvers,
estimation tooling, spatial capability, and standards interoperability.

Put as a decision rule: **COPASI** is the right call for well-mixed kinetics
needing rigorous estimation and SBML sharing; **VCell** for spatial,
microdomain, or membrane-geometry biology; the **whole-cell** approach for an
integrated, extensible, copy-number-accounted cell. The work here is governed
by the third aim, which is why it was chosen — with the first two recorded
here as the roads not taken, either of which could have produced meaningful
results.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
